family_name: MSK / Orthopedics — Pain score functional review
population: People with relevant pain-score and functional review history, findings and clinician decisions entered.
intended_setting: Partial for neuropathic pain and multimorbidity; not a universal MSK scoring or treatment protocol.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and safeguarding.

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that multimorbidity refers to the presence of 2 or more long-term health conditions, which can include: defined physical and mental health conditions such as diabetes or schizophrenia ongoing conditions such as learning disability symptom complexes such as frailty or chronic pain sensory impairment such as sight or hearing loss alcohol and substance misuse.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that multimorbidity refers to the presence of 2 or more long-term health conditions, which can include: defined physical and mental health conditions such as diabetes or schizophrenia ongoing conditions such as learning disability symptom complexes such as frailty or chronic pain sensory impairment such as sight or hearing loss alcohol and substance misuse.

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that the management of risk factors for future disease can be a major treatment burden for people with multimorbidity and should be carefully considered when optimising care.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that the management of risk factors for future disease can be a major treatment burden for people with multimorbidity and should be carefully considered when optimising care.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that the evidence for recommendations in NICE guidance on single health conditions is regularly drawn from people without multimorbidity and taking fewer prescribed regular medicines .
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that the evidence for recommendations in NICE guidance on single health conditions is regularly drawn from people without multimorbidity and taking fewer prescribed regular medicines .

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Think carefully about the risks and benefits, for people with multimorbidity, of individual treatments recommended in guidance for single health conditions. Discuss this with the patient alongside their preferences for care and treatment.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Think carefully about the risks and benefits, for people with multimorbidity, of individual treatments recommended in guidance for single health conditions. Discuss this with the patient alongside their preferences for care and treatment.

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity if the person requests it or if any of the following apply: they find it difficult to manage their treatments or day-to-day activities they receive care and support from multiple services and need additional services they have both long-term physical and mental health conditions they have frailty (see section 1.4 ) or falls they frequently seek unplanned or emergency care (see also recommendation 1.3.2) they are prescribed multiple regular medicines (see section 1.3 ).
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Identify adults who may benefit from an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity (as outlined in section 1.5 ): opportunistically during routine care proactively using electronic health records. Use the criteria in recommendation 1.2.1 to guide this.

heading_path: 1.3 How to identify people who may benefit from an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider using a validated tool such as eFI, PEONY or QAdmissions, if available in primary care electronic health records, to identify adults with multimorbidity who are at risk of adverse events such as unplanned hospital admission or admission to care homes.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider using primary care electronic health records to identify markers of increased treatment burden such as number of regular medicines a person is prescribed.

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.

evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Use an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity for adults of any age who are prescribed 15 or more regular medicines, because they are likely to be at higher risk of adverse events and drug interactions.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.3 How to identify people who may benefit from an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity for adults of any age who: are prescribed 10 to 14 regular medicines are prescribed fewer than 10 regular medicines but are at particular risk of adverse events.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not use a physical performance tool to assess frailty in a person who is acutely unwell.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Do not use a physical performance tool to assess frailty in a person who is acutely unwell.

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When assessing frailty in primary and community care settings, consider using 1 of the following: an informal assessment of gait speed (for example, time taken to answer the door, time taken to walk from the waiting room) self-reported health status (that is, 'how would you rate your health status on a scale from 0 to 10?', with scores of 6 or less indicating frailty) a formal assessment of gait speed, with more than 5 seconds to walk 4 metres indicating frailty the PRISMA-7 questionnaire, with scores of 3 and above indicating frailty.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Primary care and community care settings
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When assessing frailty in primary and community care settings, consider using 1 of the following: an informal assessment of gait speed (for example, time taken to answer the door, time taken to walk from the waiting room) self-reported health status (that is, 'how would you rate your health status on a scale from 0 to 10?', with scores of 6 or less indicating frailty) a formal assessment of gait s

heading_path: Primary care and community care settings

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When assessing frailty in hospital outpatient settings, consider using 1 of the following: self-reported health status (that is, 'how would you rate your health status on a scale from 0 to 10?', with scores of 6 or less indicating frailty) the 'Timed Up and Go' test, with times of more than 12 seconds indicating frailty a formal assessment of gait speed, with more than 5 seconds to walk 4 metres indicating frailty the PRISMA‑7 questionnaire, with scores of 3 and above indicating frailty self-reported physical activity, with frailty indicated by scores of 56 or less for men and 59 or less for women using the Physical Activity Scale for the Elderly.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When assessing frailty in hospital outpatient settings, consider using 1 of the following: self-reported health status (that is, 'how would you rate your health status on a scale from 0 to 10?', with scores of 6 or less indicating frailty) the 'Timed Up and Go' test, with times of more than 12 seconds indicating frailty a formal assessment of gait speed, with more than 5 seconds to walk 4 metres i

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When offering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity, focus on: how the person's health conditions and their treatments interact and how this affects quality of life the person's individual needs, preferences for treatments, health priorities, lifestyle and goals the benefits and risks of following recommendations from guidance on single health conditions improving quality of life by reducing treatment burden, adverse events, and unplanned care improving coordination of care across services.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.5 Principles of an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Follow these steps when delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity: Discuss the purpose of an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity (see recommendation 1.6.2 ). Establish disease and treatment burden (see recommendations 1.6.3 to 1.6.5 ). Establish patient goals, values and priorities (see recommendations 1.6.6 to 1.6.8 ). Review medicines and other treatments taking into account evidence of likely benefits and harms for the individual patient and outcomes important to the person (see recommendations 1.6.9 to 1.6.16 ). Agree an individualised management plan with the person (see recommendation 1.6.17 ), including: goals and plans for future care (including advance care planning) who is responsible for coordination of care how the individualised management plan and the responsibility for coordination of care is communicated to all professionals and
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Follow the recommendations in the NICE guideline on patient experience in adult NHS services , which provides guidance on knowing the patient as an individual, tailoring healthcare services for each patient, continuity of care and relationships, and enabling patients to actively participate in their care.

heading_path: 1.6 Delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Follow the recommendations in the NICE guideline on patient experience in adult NHS services , which provides guidance on knowing the patient as an individual, tailoring healthcare services for each patient, continuity of care and relationships, and enabling patients to actively participate in their care.

heading_path: 1.6 Delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Discuss with the person the purpose of the approach to care, that is, to improve quality of life. This might include reducing treatment burden and optimising care and support by identifying: ways of maximising benefit from existing treatments treatments that could be stopped because of limited benefit treatments and follow-up arrangements with a high burden medicines with a higher risk of adverse events (for example, falls, gastrointestinal bleeding, acute kidney injury) non-pharmacological treatments as possible alternatives to some medicines alternative arrangements for follow-up to coordinate or optimise the number of appointments.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Establish disease burden by talking to people about how their health problems affect their day-to-day life. Include a discussion of: mental health how disease burden affects their wellbeing how their health problems interact and how this affects quality of life.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.6 Delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Establish treatment burden by talking to people about how treatments for their health problems affect their day-to-day life. Include in the discussion: the number and type of healthcare appointments a person has and where these take place the number and type of medicines a person is taking and how often any harms from medicines non-pharmacological treatments such as diets, exercise programmes and psychological treatments any effects of treatment on their mental health or wellbeing.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be alert to the possibility of: depression and anxiety (consider identifying, assessing and managing these conditions in line with the NICE guideline on common mental health problems ) chronic pain and the need to assess this and the adequacy of pain management.

evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Clarify with the patient whether and how they would like their partner, family members and/or carers to be involved in key decisions about the management of their conditions. Review this regularly. If the patient agrees, share information with their partner, family members and/or carers. [This recommendation is adapted from the NICE guideline on patient experience in adult NHS services .]
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.6 Delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity
Establishing patient goals, values and priorities
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Clarify with the patient whether and how they would like their partner, family members and/or carers to be involved in key decisions about the management of their conditions. Review this regularly. If the patient agrees, share information with their partner, family members and/or carers. [This recommendation is adapted from the NICE guideline on patient experience in adult NHS services .]

heading_path: 1.6 Delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity
Establishing patient goals, values and priorities

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Encourage people with multimorbidity to clarify what is important to them, including their personal goals, values and priorities. These may include: maintaining their independence undertaking paid or voluntary work, taking part in social activities and playing an active part in family life preventing specific adverse outcomes (for example, stroke) reducing harms from medicines reducing treatment burden lengthening life.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Explore the person's attitudes to their treatments and the potential benefits and harms of those treatments. evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Ask the person if treatments intended to relieve symptoms are providing benefits or causing harms. If the person is unsure of benefit or is experiencing harms from a treatment: discuss reducing or stopping the treatment plan a review to monitor effects of any changes made and decide whether any further changes to treatments are needed (including restarting a treatment).

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Tell a person who has been taking bisphosphonate for osteoporosis for at least 3 years that there is no consistent evidence of: further benefit from continuing bisphosphonate for another 3 years harms from stopping bisphosphonate after 3 years of treatment. Discuss stopping bisphosphonate after 3 years and include patient choice, fracture risk and life expectancy in the discussion.

heading_path: 1.6 Delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: After a discussion of disease and treatment burden and the person's, personal goals, values and priorities, develop and agree an individualised management plan with the person. Agree what will be recorded and what actions will be taken. These could include: starting, stopping or changing medicines and non-pharmacological treatments prioritising healthcare appointments anticipating possible changes to health and wellbeing assigning responsibility for coordination of care and ensuring this is communicated to other healthcare professionals and services other areas the person considers important to them arranging a follow-up and review of decisions made. Share copies of the management plan in an accessible format with the person and (with the person's permission) other people involved in care (including healthcare professionals, a partner, family members and/or carers).
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.

evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Multimorbidity refers to the presence of 2 or more long-term health conditions, which can include: defined physical and mental health conditions such as diabetes or schizophrenia ongoing conditions such as learning disability symptom complexes such as frailty or chronic pain sensory impairment such as sight or hearing loss alcohol and substance misuse. The management of risk factors for future disease can be a major treatment burden for people with multimorbidity and should be carefully considered when optimising care. This guideline covers the optimisation of care for: adults with 2 or more long-term physical health conditions adults with 1 or more mental health condition and at least 1 physical health condition.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Multimorbidity refers to the presence of 2 or more long-term health conditions, which can include: defined physical and mental health conditions such as diabetes or schizophrenia ongoing conditions such as learning disability symptom complexes such as frailty or chronic pain sensory impairment such as sight or hearing loss alcohol and substance misuse. The management of risk factors for future dis

evidence_statements.population: Adults with two or more long-term conditions, including frailty, falls, sensory impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, and treatment burden.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and community care with person-centred multimorbidity review.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE geriatric and primary-care adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: An approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity involves personalised assessment and the development of an individualised management plan. The aim is to improve quality of life by reducing treatment burden, adverse events, and unplanned or uncoordinated care. The approach takes account of a person's individual needs, preferences for treatments, health priorities and lifestyle. It aims to improve coordination of care across services, particularly if this has become fragmented.
evidence_statements.source_title: Multimorbidity: clinical assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: An individualised management plan is a management plan covering clinical aspects of a person's care, such as the medicines they are taking and the services they are attending. It includes information about which areas of care are most important to the person and whether treatments have been stopped to reduce treatment burden.

evidence_statements.setting: Non-specialist assessment, treatment review, and specialist referral.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off‑label use), professional guidelines, standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and safeguarding. The following guidance is based on the best available evidence. The full guideline gives details of the methods and the evidence used to develop the guidance.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider referring the person to a specialist pain service and/or a condition-specific service at any stage, including at initial presentation and at the regular clinical reviews (see recommendation 1.1.6), if: they have severe pain or their pain significantly limits their lifestyle, daily activities (including sleep disturbance) and participation or their underlying health condition has deteriorated.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Offer a choice of amitriptyline, duloxetine, gabapentin or pregabalin as initial treatment for neuropathic pain (except trigeminal neuralgia). See additional information for more on duloxetine, gabapentin and pregabalin.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If the initial treatment is not effective or is not tolerated, offer 1 of the remaining 3 drugs, and consider switching again if the second and third drugs tried are also not effective or not tolerated.

heading_path: Neuropathic pain in adults: pharmacological management in non-specialist settings

evidence_statements.population: Adults with neuropathic pain managed in non-specialist settings.
evidence_statements.setting: Non-specialist assessment, treatment review, and specialist referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England, United Kingdom

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not start the following to treat neuropathic pain in non-specialist settings , unless advised by a specialist to do so: cannabis sativa extract capsaicin patch lacosamide lamotrigine levetiracetam morphine oxcarbazepine topiramate (follow the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) safety advice on the use of topiramate ) tramadol (this is referring to long-term use; see recommendation 1.1.10 for short-term use) venlafaxine valproate (follow the MHRA safety advice on valproate use in women and girls , valproate use in people younger than 55 years and valproate use in men ). [amended 2018] Also follow the MHRA safety advice on anti-epileptic drugs in pregnancy .
evidence_statements.source_title: Neuropathic pain in adults: pharmacological management in non-specialist settings

heading_path: Neuropathic pain in adults: pharmacological management in non-specialist settings
Treatments that should not be used
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Do not start the following to treat neuropathic pain in non-specialist settings , unless advised by a specialist to do so: cannabis sativa extract capsaicin patch lacosamide lamotrigine levetiracetam morphine oxcarbazepine topiramate (follow the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) safety advice on the use of topiramate ) tramadol (this is referring to long-term use; see reco

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If initial treatment with carbamazepine is not effective, is not tolerated or is contraindicated, consider seeking expert advice from a specialist and consider early referral to a specialist pain service or a condition-specific service.
